title: Time Percentage Calculator
tagline: Calculate percentages of time durations or find partial and total time from a known percentage.
intro: Select a calculation type, enter the known values, and get an instant, accurate result.

type.findPercentage_desc: Given a total time and a partial time, find what percentage the partial time is of the total.
type.findPartial_desc: Given a total time and a percentage, find the duration that corresponds to that percentage.
type.findTotal_desc: Given a partial time and the percentage it represents, find the full total time.

error.invalid: Please enter valid numbers in all required fields.
error.divideByZero: Total time or percentage cannot be zero for this calculation.

result.heading: Result
result.findPercentage: The partial time is {result} of the total time.

about.heading: About the Time Percentage Calculator
about.body:
Time is one of the most fundamental quantities we measure, yet calculating percentages of time durations is surprisingly tricky when hours, minutes, and seconds are involved. The Time Percentage Calculator removes the mental arithmetic by handling all three common time-percentage problems in one place.

The first mode — Find Percentage — answers the question: what fraction of a total duration has elapsed or been allocated? To solve it, the calculator converts both the total time and the partial time into a common unit (seconds), then divides the partial by the total and multiplies by one hundred. For example, if you have worked 2 hours of an 8-hour shift, you convert 2 hours to 7,200 seconds and 8 hours to 28,800 seconds, divide, and multiply by 100 to get 25%. This mode is ideal for tracking work-shift completion, monitoring project milestones, measuring how far through a video or podcast you are, or computing what share of a daily exercise goal you have reached.

The second mode — Find Partial Time — solves the inverse: given a total duration and a percentage, what is the corresponding partial duration? The formula is simply partial_seconds = (percentage / 100) × total_seconds, converted back into hours, minutes, and seconds. Project managers use this when they want to allocate 15% of a 40-hour sprint to a planning phase, or teachers use it to set aside 20% of a 90-minute lesson for review questions. The calculator returns hours, minutes, and seconds so you can immediately schedule the block on a calendar.

The third mode — Find Total Time — reverses the second: given a partial duration and the percentage it represents, what is the full total? The formula is total_seconds = (partial_seconds / percentage) × 100. If you spent 45 minutes exercising and that represents 75% of your daily goal, the total goal works out to 60 minutes. This mode is useful for deadline estimation (you know how long a sub-task took and its percentage share of the whole project) and for reverse-engineering schedules.

Behind the scenes the calculator stores all time values as total seconds to avoid rounding errors when crossing minute or hour boundaries. Results are converted back to hours, minutes, and seconds using integer division, so you always get a clean H h M m S s display rather than a decimal fraction of an hour.

Common pitfalls to watch for: make sure you enter the larger duration as the total and the smaller as the partial when using Find Percentage, because nothing stops you from entering a partial time larger than the total — you would simply get a percentage above 100, which is valid mathematically but may not match your intent. Also note that percentages are applied to the total in a linear way; compounding (e.g., two successive percentage reductions) requires running the calculator twice.

examples.heading: Time percentage calculator examples
examples.intro: Four real-world scenarios illustrating each mode in action.

items.input: 2 h 0 m 0 s worked out of 8 h 0 m 0 s shift
items.note: Find Percentage mode. 2 hours ÷ 8 hours × 100 = 25%. A quarter of the work shift is complete.

items.input: 15% of a 40 h 0 m 0 s project
items.note: Find Partial Time mode. 0.15 × 40 hours = 6 hours. Allocate 6 hours to the planning phase.

items.input: 0 h 45 m 0 s is 75% of the daily goal
items.note: Find Total Time mode. 45 minutes ÷ 0.75 = 60 minutes = 1 hour total daily goal.

items.input: 0 h 45 m 15 s watched of 2 h 15 m 30 s video
items.note: Find Percentage mode. 2715 s ÷ 8130 s × 100 ≈ 33.52%. Just over a third of the video has been watched.

howto.heading: How to use the Time Percentage Calculator
steps: Choose the calculation type: Find Percentage, Find Partial Time, or Find Total Time.
Enter the known time durations using the hours, minutes, and seconds fields. Leave unused fields as 0.
Enter the percentage value if your chosen mode requires it.
Click Calculate. The result appears instantly below the inputs.
Click Reset to clear all fields and start a new calculation.

faq.heading: Time Percentage Calculator FAQ
faq.q1: How do I calculate what percentage one time duration is of another?
faq.q1_a: Use the Find Percentage mode. Enter the larger duration as the total and the smaller as the partial. The calculator converts both to seconds, divides partial by total, and multiplies by 100. For example, 30 minutes out of 2 hours (120 minutes) is 30 ÷ 120 × 100 = 25%.
faq.q2: What happens if I enter a partial time larger than the total time?
faq.q2_a: The calculator will return a percentage greater than 100%, which is mathematically valid. This would mean you have exceeded the total — for instance, working overtime beyond the scheduled shift. If this is not your intent, swap the total and partial values.
faq.q3: Can I calculate what 15% of a 40-hour project is?
faq.q3_a: Yes. Use Find Partial Time mode, enter 40 hours as the total time, and enter 15 as the percentage. The result is 6 hours exactly. This is useful for allocating phases of a project or scheduling blocks of a study plan.
faq.q4: How is the result time displayed?
faq.q4_a: Results are shown in hours, minutes, and seconds — for example, 1 h 30 m 45 s. The calculator uses integer arithmetic for hours and minutes so there is no ambiguity about rounding. Seconds are rounded to two decimal places when fractional.
faq.q5: Why does the Find Total Time mode require a non-zero percentage?
faq.q5_a: Dividing by zero is undefined in mathematics, and zero percent would imply the partial time represents none of the total — making the total unknowable. If you enter 0% in that mode the calculator will show an error message rather than return an incorrect result.
faq.q6: Is this calculator useful for video progress or podcast tracking?
faq.q6_a: Absolutely. Use Find Percentage mode with the total video or podcast length as the total time and the elapsed time as the partial time. You will get the exact percentage watched or listened to, which can be useful for resuming content or reporting to colleagues.
